sentences.text: Yes.
As I said before, danger.
Danger was the first time we saw this German outfit.
And we could not move anymore freely.
We had to hide, or watch out who is coming.
But then all of a sudden, we find out that people were taken away.
And they did not return.
I mean, it is probably no one from [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] or other things that people talk about.
Death-- there were private firms who took some workers.
And they were interested to keep them, not to let them go.
So in this-- in [POLISH] was, it was a city, in which they had a, a steel mill.
That's what it was like-- no, they were melting steel.
And they needed workers, of course.
And, of course the Germans took over this, [BACKGROUND NOISE] this outfit.
So, so they took any-- they took the young people now, what they were in the city.
And they made them work in this steel mill, steel whatever-- [NON-ENGLISH], they called it at the time.
So I-- we were a lot of young people, which we signed up to work there.
Because when you worked for an outfit like this, at least your life was safe.
You went to work for the day.
And you came back.
In the evening, you came back home.
So in the beginning, we signed up, so we were safe to-- we knew that nobody-- we had identification that we are working at this factory, that is melting steel for the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so we were able to breathe a little.
Because we knew that our life was not in danger.
But all of a sudden, we found out that the other cities-- we could not, of course, we could not move from one city to another.
We could not-- we could not have any connection with other people.
So we, we were told that some cities the people are just taken away, and they disappeared.
And some [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] news came out that those people are being put away in camps, and they were held there.
In the beginning, we didn't know that they were exterminated, they were killed.
But sometimes, all of a sudden, we found out that people escaped from these trains, which they were taking them.
And they were-- they told us that it is a system now that even if you don't work and if you're not [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] useful to them, they are going to your chances of surviving are very slim.
And, of course, there were so many.
They started-- right away, they started to cut off the Jews, to make, to make a ghetto in the city.
Before, you lived in the whole city.
And now, they-- all of a sudden, they said that they are going to take away a lot of those elderly people who did not work, they're going to take them to a Jewish city.
They're going to make Jewish cities, where all the Jews can live there in freedom and at ease.
Yes, it was like this.
But in the meanwhile, they squeezed the city, the Jews from the old-- in just a few blocks, a fewer streets.
So you were limited.
They call it a ghetto.
It was policed.
So we realized that it's getting tighter and tighter.
And they took away those elderly people.
And we never heard of them, because it was just a hoax.
It was-- but people will take any-- anything to save themselves.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So then we realized that it's getting worse.
But there was nothing, there was no organization.
Especially in my case, I was always with religious kind of people.
And those people were never aggressive.
They were never trying to-- we always thought God will provide.
It will happen.
I mean, as they say in Hebrew, [HEBREW].
It was-- usually it would not be lost.
But unfortunately, it got worse and worse.
And all of a sudden, they took all the Jews.
They took-- one night they didn't let us go home from the factory that we worked.
And they kept us there overnight.
And during the night, they take out all the Jews from this [POLISH], and they took them away.
They put-- loaded them up on trains.
And they went to [NON-ENGLISH] or Treblinka, it was.
And we got in the morning, we were-- we came back by probably more than one day.
It took two days.
And they gave us one block for all the workers who worked in this factory, that we should live in this block.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the city, all of a sudden was empty, it was.
We found out the atrocities, what happened.
Because a lot of people were hiding themselves.
They were hiding in graves and all kinds of bunkers.
And after that, they sort of sneaked out and came into this block that we were there.
And they told us what happened, the atrocities and the way they took the people.

And when we walked in the morning, we saw on the snow-- the snow was still full of blood, which they told us this was the, the, the blood of people-- of elderly people who could not walk as fast or that didn't walk in the line the way they want them to-- so they just shot them in the middle of the road.
And they-- so we followed-- we saw the snow and the blood, full of, of Jewish blood.
It was stepped on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] And so we stayed in this block or two, it was, for another while.
And then they finally built a concentration camp, a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] outside this factory where we worked, with barbed wire, with electric barbed wire.
And they put some barracks.
And one day, they didn't allows us to go back to the city at all.
And we were held there.
We put on, you know, we were-- had to live there from this day on.
It was probably in 1941, which we stayed, we remained in these barracks, watched by the Ukrainians, which they helped the Germans, more than the Poles, even.
Well, it was the Ostrowiec, [POLISH], the Ostrowiec.
It, it actually, it was a working, a labor camp.
But it was watched by electric wire and the Ukrainians.
And it was in walking distance to the factory where we worked.
And sometimes, they even brought in from the other camps, they brought in people to-- because they needed more workers in this, in this camp-- in this factory.
Oh, yes.
There were--
I would say-- well, it was all kinds of people.
First of all, in this camp, in the beginning, there were only the people that worked in this factory.
But there were a lot of people from the city, who worked in different kind of works.
Like when they-- they worked for building-- making bricks.
In the city, there were all kinds of suppliers.
They worked for the Germans, making shoes-- all kinds of projects.
And these people were also kept there, while they send away the Jews.
So after these Jews were-- the, the city was free of, of, of the Jewish population, they brought all these people which they kept in this camp.
So all of a sudden, we saw there was a lot of-- besides the workers from this, from this steel mill, they also brought the rest of the Jews, which they worked all over the city.
So let's say we were probably a few thousand young people in this camp.
And every, every morning, we divided-- we went to the work, to work under guard, of course, to this factory.
And everyone had his own place, where they went to work in the morning.
So the camp was almost-- during the day, it was nobody there.
Well, again, I did not-- yeah, it's a matter of fact, my aunt-- this woman which, she somehow had some papers that she worked for somebody.
She did not, but she had some papers.
So she was left, and she stayed behind in this camp, an elderly lady.
She was quite sick, but somehow she managed to.
And otherwise, we only had some friends which I knew from the city, young people from the yeshiva, from the school, which they also managed to stay and to, to get their paper-- working papers.
So we, those were the only people.
And, of course, in a place like this, we all became close friends.
We all became very, very, you know, close.
Well, it's unbelievable.
But while we were in these camps-- well, in this [POLISH], [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it comes to my mind, we all of a sudden, we, we start building a cement factory, next to the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I became head engineer, to help the people.
Because I was able to always try to figure out things.
And so there were a lot of people who could not even handle a screw or a screwdriver.
But I was able to manage to adapt myself.
And I was sort of helping the youngsters or even elderly people who were there.
But we were able to continue.
Well, kosher was a problem.
So you didn't eat no meat, but still, the rest of the food, it was very difficult.
But we were still-- we organized a synagogue in the concentration camp-- in this camp.
We were able to it came the holidays, we were still able to-- so the non-Jews, they brought us some flour on Pesach, I remember.
We baked matzos inside a oven, which we built for to make cement.
But before the oven was workable, we baked inside.
We put a fire, we baked, baked matzos and we ate.
And [BACKGROUND NOISE] even at work, we were sometimes were working late at night.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you wouldn't go home-- go home.
You wouldn't go back to camp, until you finished your work.
And there it was, Pesach, even on Pesach night, at the Seder.
And we just had our ration, whatever, at the time it was, a piece of bread.
And as I remembered, I still, I still-- I remember some of the Haggadah by heart.
And I used to sit with those people, a lot of youngsters sitting down and give them a little pep-- pep talk.
And just looking to the future, that some day-- it was not meant that this should be the end.
So we were able to once in a while to even to, to have a concert.
Because there were some people musicians, some good musicians, which they managed to hold back.
And they organized-- we organized between ourselves.
We had once in a while some music playing.
And then if you were young, you sort of hold onto life.
And after you came back from the, from work, you were able still to sit down.
And sometimes, you would play cards or sometimes, you know, to go to a-- listen to music or to go to even to say a prayer.
Well, well, I'm not talking now about concentration camp.
Those are the labor camps.
These labor camps were geared to protect the work-- the people, because they needed our service.

But there's one-- to tell you the conditions the way they was, there is one-- we used to come-- in the morning, they used to get out of the camps, the barracks, and stand up, stay in line, to be counted.
And then you marched off to work.
Coming back, it was the same thing.
You had to stand in line to be counted.
And one day, we came back from work.
And they lined us up.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And then they called a name, which it was right in my barrack, only two seats away from the bunker that I slept.
And they ask him, who sleeps in this and this place.
And there were young fellow walked out.
I'll never forget his name, his name was Jeremiah Zaczinski.
I'll never forget this [INAUDIBLE].
The reason I remember him, because he was my closest friend.
And he was not-- because he had a mind like a-- now, today, I understand.
At the time, we didn't know there was such a thing as an IQ.
He, he could-- he had a photographic mind.
And whenever the teacher used to say something, he used to just automatically, he never forget it.
And we always, whenever we missed something, we came to him, and he remembered.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they called up, and he walks out.
And he says, yes, it's me who sleeps there.
They put him the middle of the street, middle of-- in the center, and they shot him.
They shot him dead.
Why?
Later we found out that on the top of his bunk, where he was sleeping, between the cracks, they found a, a $5 bill that somebody put it there.
He didn't have any $5.
He didn't know what it meant.
But somehow, somebody was able to put it there.

And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so, and as a matter of fact, we buried-- we buried him right outside, you know, the gate, without even no big deal, and-- So if you want to know how life was there, while we were working, I mean, we had freedom inside the factory.
But we had to do your work.
Like, I was working at a steel mill.
And I had to-- where they, they, they melt steel.

And being a yeshiva bocher and being so-- and to, to many times, you know, you had to-- when they opened the, the, the door, when the steel is boiling, and you had to throw in some raw material, by the time you got there, you throw in the shovel with everything, because you couldn't hold onto it.
And then when you had to rebuild those ovens, they used to send us in while the, the ovens were still boiling hot.
And it was-- I don't know if anybody's familiar how does, how the steel is boiled.
But the accumulation of the, of, of the, of the stuff, they have to be cleaned once in a while.
And this was our work.
And it was unhuman, the type of work that you had to do.
And then most of the time, it was this unloading of steel pieces, from wagons.
And there were sometimes heavy metals, which was impossible to, to carry.
And you had to devise such-- all kinds of things how to make it work.
Because otherwise, your chances of survival would have been nil, would have been impossible.
Yes, it was hard for, for, this type of life.
Besides, you-- you know, it broke your, your mind.
It broke your spirit.

It-- you-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was always, you know, no matter how strong you are, you asked yourself where is, where is anything that you were so convinced all your life, anything that you are, you will-- you think it's always somebody is interested in, if it's a high power or whatever, there's-- and we saw the, the, the atrocities and the way people were just-- you know, the best of people, the best in the world, the best what, what what life could put together, they were just slaughtered-- young children.
I had a teacher which, he had a child, a baby, after so many years of not having children.
And when the Germans came, and when they, they, they came, they had to send away all the people.
And they wanted-- they took him.
And they wanted to take away his baby, and he fight, he fought.
He didn't let them take his only little-- maybe a year old or so.
So in front of his eyes, they took the baby.
And they threw it, and they smashed his head against the wall.
They gave it to him.
They said, here, you want your child?
Here it is.
So when you see those things, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sometimes you know, you lose your courage, your lose your, your strength, what keeps you, kept you.
And it's very difficult to continue.
But it's not that the suffering that you had not having enough, not to have freedom, or not to have enough food.
It is just inside that, that they--
What they say about Amalek, Amalek, they say don't forget.
The system of Amalek, it's not-- they say they want to break your spirit inside, the same as-- not long ago we had Hanukkah.
You say to the [HEBREW].
[HEBREW] means two meaning, two meanings.
It means what happened.
It also means they wanted to cool you, [HEBREW].
They wanted to chill you.
They wanted to get off-- to take away-- away your, your strength and your spirit.
Well, it's amazing.
It's a, it is-- you can't help it.
Life goes on.
And all of a sudden, when you find yourself, that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, in between, when I-- from there, they-- I had to go to-- they took us to Auschwitz.
And this was a-- from [POLISH] to Auschwitz, it was a real-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I wouldn't say experience.
But it was a-- the most, the next to hell or even more than that.
Because they put us all in trains.
And it was in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we were traveling through Prague in open boxcars.
And we were so squeezed that we could not turn even.
And all of a sudden, the only place we could find a relief was-- because a lot of people in these boxcars, they just perished, they died.
So what we did is we just put them down on the side, and we sat on them.
This was the only place we could-- This was in 1944.
When we left [POLISH] and we came to Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- and we traveled probably about at least two days in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 40 degrees, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cold weather.
Without food, without anything.
The only what-- the only thing you could get is when the train stopped, we jumped down to get a little hot water from the locomotive, with the steam.
It was shooting out, a little hot water.
And when we came to Auschwitz, [BACKGROUND NOISE] they didn't know what to do with us.
So they kept us in a barrack.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But we were 2,500 people, exactly.
They kept us in a barrack for about 36 hours.
And at night, we were able to see the action outside.
That is, we are next to the crematorium.
So we knew that this is it.
This is the end.
And a lot of people committed suicide.
A lot of people cut their, their, their wrists.
And I was able still to accumulate a little, to get a little strength and talk to those people and to minimize death.
How can you tell somebody that death is not the worst thing?
Of course, it isn't.
But when you are young, life is everything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But some people, just they couldn't stand it.
They couldn't hold on.
So we were-- and all of a sudden, they opened the doors.
And when we got into the crematorium, we thought we-- it was, it wasn't as bad that you go in and instead of gas, water came.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we couldn't believe it.
Then when we came out, of course, we had new clothing and everything.
We looked different than we looked in this labor camp.
So we were told that the night before, it was the night when they burned all the gypsies which they had in Auschwitz.
And the last minute, there came an order that they need some workers.
So instead of putting us to crematorium, they took all the gypsies, and they emptied out and they burned them all.
And they gave us their quarters.

As a matter of fact, when we came next day to Birkenau, which it was before Auschwitz, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there we were with beautiful blankets and all kinds of ornaments and all kinds of-- we didn't know what is this?
They said that those are the quarters from the gypsies.
They used to live here yesterday.
And then where I got this number.
This number I got when I got there, after this-- Here, if you want to know.
People ask me, is this my telephone?
I have to-- this is upside down.
But it is B-5278.
I'll tell you, I tried to play this number in the lotto.
It didn't work.
So sometimes this was my lotto, that I got this number.
This is my lottery number.
Yes, you're-- this is a wonderful question.
Because it's the truth.
I think this came from this--
